Houston, TX

Kasey Hodge

Houston · 27


Every chapter of my life is the same story in a different arena. Boy Scouts when other kids quit. Campouts in the rain. An Eagle Scout service project—dirty hands before the ceremony. Peers elected me to the Order of the Arrow. You can’t campaign for that.

I learned to fly the right way. Solo first. Then instruments—actual weather, not just clear-sky weekends. Became a licensed electrician not because I had to, but because I wanted to understand the craft before running a business built on it. The through-line is simple: I do the long way through. Every time.

The copilot

Blu, my girl. She’s a better copilot than most humans I know and she flies more miles a year than people who buy first class.

“She’s planned her day around it. She’s not wrong.”

The yellow Lab in the throwback shot was her predecessor. The Great Dane is a more recent development. The cabin is not getting smaller.

Earned, not awarded

“Am an Eagle Scout. Can start a fire in the woods… still Google recipes.”

I grew up in Scouting. Not as a badge-collecting exercise—as a genuine education in what it means to finish something hard. The Eagle Scout rank requires years of work, a leadership service project you design and execute, and a board review. I earned mine. So did maybe 4% of the boys who start.

The left photo is the project—hands dirty, mid-build, nobody watching. The right is the ceremony. Both are part of the story. The ceremony means nothing without what came first.

The Order of the Arrow is Scouting’s national honor society. Induction is by election from your troop—peers only. You don’t nominate yourself.

Philmont, New Mexico

Philmont Scout Ranch in New Mexico. Ten days. Seventy-five miles. Full pack. My dad and I did this together. Forest floor on the left, mountaintop on the right—same trip, different altitude.

The work

I co-built our family’s HVAC and electrical business alongside my father—Houston and DFW markets, real infrastructure for real buildings. I got my electrician’s license not because the role required it, but because I wanted to know what the guys on my crew know. You don’t lead a trade you haven’t practiced.

Operations Director. No MBA. No venture capital. I chose work over school, which isn’t the path people expect. But the tradeoff was ten years of real experience while everyone else was getting a degree. I’d rather build something than talk about building something.

Left seat

I learned to fly the right way. Solo first, then instruments. Flying in actual weather—not just clear-sky weekends—teaches you to trust the panel when your gut says something different. It’s a practice more than a skill.

Where it started — Tidal Aviation

First solo.

First solo.

Instrument rating
Laminar Holdings HondaJet

Our family holds a HondaJet through Laminar Holdings. Aviation teaches you to respect systems, stay calm when things get interesting, and always have a plan for what happens next. Those lessons follow you back to the ground.

Off the clock

Running—usually at night, usually alone, usually when I need to think. Finish lines included when the calendar allows. Dad shows up for those too.

I keep a pretty small circle and a pretty private life. Not because there’s a strategy behind it. I just don’t feel the need to narrate things as they happen.

The people

My dad and I built this business together. My mom is the reason both of us know what we’re doing on the ground. Same family, same values, same stubborn work ethic.

People ask me sometimes what the plan is. Build bigger? Go public? Move to a coast? I don’t really think like that. I think about whether the people around me are good, whether the work is honest, and whether I’m better this year than last year. The rest sorts itself out.

“Am an Eagle Scout. Can start a fire in the woods… still Google recipes.”

My dad taught me that the way you do anything is the way you do everything. I haven’t found a reason to argue with that yet.

The work comes before the title. It always has.

Kasey Hodge · Houston, TX
